does: This paper proposes U-LiRA+ (Unlearning Likelihood Ratio Attack+), a rigorous auditing method for evaluating the effectiveness of textual unlearning in language models, and develops Textual Unlearning Leakage Attack (TULA) to exploit unlearning mechanisms. The authors demonstrate that existing unlearning approaches fail to truly remove memorized text: unlearned passages can still be detected with high confidence after unlearning, and the unlearning process itself creates new vulnerabilities that expose sensitive information through membership inference and data reconstruction attacks. They evaluate both black-box and white-box attack scenarios, revealing that deployed unlearning mechanisms may paradoxically leak more information about texts they claim to have forgotten.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature on machine unlearning and privacy risks in language models. It responds to recent efforts enabling efficient textual unlearning as a defense against memorization, following concerns raised by works studying data extraction and privacy leakage from LMs. The paper extends prior membership inference attack methodology and unlearning evaluation frameworks to the specific domain of textual data, combining techniques from privacy auditing with model-specific attack design. It also connects to broader research on the limitations and false guarantees of privacy-preserving machine learning techniques, questioning whether conceptually sound approaches achieve their intended goals when implemented in practice.
enables: This work opens directions for developing genuinely effective unlearning mechanisms that provide provable privacy guarantees rather than superficial mitigation. It suggests the need for new unlearning algorithms that account for and defend against the specific attack vectors identified, potentially drawing on techniques from differential privacy or information-theoretic approaches. The auditing methodology itself enables systematic evaluation of future unlearning proposals and provides a framework for studying privacy risks in other model-editing or knowledge-removal scenarios. More broadly, it motivates investigation into fundamental limits of unlearning—whether and under what conditions true forgetting is achievable—and establishes benchmarks for measuring genuine privacy improvement in language model deployment.
